title: Interleaving String
problem_statement: Given strings s1, s2, and s3, determine whether s3 is formed by an interleaving of s1 and s2. An interleaving preserves the relative order of characters from s1 and s2.

examples.explanation: s3 can be formed by interleaving s1 and s2 while preserving the order of each string.
The relative order of characters from s1 or s2 cannot produce s3.

intuition: At each prefix length i and j, decide whether the next character of s3 can come from s1 or s2, and track which prefix pairs are valid.

approaches.name: Recursive Brute Force
approaches.description: Try taking the next character of s3 from either s1 or s2, recursing on remaining prefixes. Backtrack on invalid choices.
approaches.why_not: Exponential because each position may branch into two choices without memoization.

approaches.name: Memoized Recursion (Top-Down DP)
approaches.description: Memoize valid prefix combinations (i,j) to avoid revisiting the same state.
approaches.why_not: Bottom-up is easier to implement iteratively, but this is correct and efficient.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up Dynamic Programming
approaches.description: Use a 2D DP table where dp[i][j] indicates whether s3[0..i+j-1] can be formed by s1[0..i-1] and s2[0..j-1].
approaches.why_not: This is the standard optimal solution. Space can be optimized to O(n) if needed.

interview_qa.a: It means that the first i chars of s1 and first j chars of s2 can interleave to form the first i+j chars of s3.

interview_qa.q: How do you choose between s1 and s2?
interview_qa.a: Check if the next char in s3 matches the next char in s1 and dp[i-1][j] is true, or if it matches the next char in s2 and dp[i][j-1] is true.
dp[0][0] is true, and the first row/column depends only on matching the prefix of s2 or s1 with s3.

interview_qa.q: Can this be solved with O(n) space?
interview_qa.a: Yes, by using a 1D array over s2 length and updating it row by row.
